title: Motor Vehicles Act: Duties of driver after accident
citation: Motor Vehicles Act, 1988 (India)
text: When a road accident occurs, the driver is expected to stop, assist the injured person in obtaining medical care, share identifying information, and report the incident to the police as required by law. Leaving the scene without complying with these duties can trigger criminal and regulatory consequences.

title: Good Samaritan protections and emergency aid
citation: Parmanand Katara v. Union of India (1989) and subsequent Good Samaritan framework
text: Indian legal policy encourages immediate medical assistance to accident victims. A bystander helping an injured person should not be harassed for unnecessary procedural reasons; saving life first is a key legal and constitutional principle.

title: Rash and negligent driving consequences
citation: Indian criminal law principles on rash/negligent driving and causing hurt/death
text: If a person drives rashly or negligently and causes injury or death, criminal prosecution can follow. Punishment can increase with gravity of harm, and courts consider surrounding facts such as speed, conduct after accident, and evidence quality.

title: Motor Accident Claims Tribunal compensation route
citation: Motor Vehicles Act compensation framework (MACT)
text: Victims and dependents can seek monetary compensation through the Motor Accident Claims Tribunal. Key documents usually include FIR details, medical records, proof of income, death/injury proof, and evidence linking accident to the offending vehicle.

title: Sarla Verma compensation principles
citation: Sarla Verma v. Delhi Transport Corporation (2009)
text: The Supreme Court clarified structured principles for calculating compensation in fatal accident claims, including use of income, future prospects in relevant contexts, and multiplier-based assessment for dependency loss.

title: Pranay Sethi on future prospects and conventional heads
citation: National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Pranay Sethi (2017)
text: The Court refined compensation calculation by standardizing treatment of future prospects and conventional heads such as loss of estate, funeral expenses, and consortium, improving consistency in award determination.

title: Mandatory FIR for cognizable offenses
citation: Lalita Kumari v. Government of Uttar Pradesh (2013)
text: For cognizable offenses, registration of FIR is generally mandatory. Preliminary inquiry is limited to specific categories and cannot become a routine barrier where law requires immediate registration.

title: Arrest and detention safeguards
citation: D.K. Basu v. State of West Bengal (1997)
text: Arrest and detention must follow constitutional safeguards, including procedural transparency and protection against abuse. Families and legal representatives should be able to trace custody events and documentation.

title: Fair procedure under Article 21
citation: Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
text: Any state action affecting life or personal liberty must satisfy fair, just, and reasonable procedure. Arbitrary deprivation is inconsistent with constitutional guarantees.

title: Basic structure doctrine
citation: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala (1973)
text: Parliament can amend the Constitution but cannot destroy its basic structure. This doctrine protects foundational constitutional principles from total abrogation.

title: Right to privacy
citation: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy (Retd.) v. Union of India (2017)
text: Privacy is a fundamental right under the Constitution. State or private intrusions into personal data and autonomy are assessed against legality, necessity, and proportionality standards.

title: Workplace sexual harassment safeguards
citation: Vishaka v. State of Rajasthan (1997)
text: Before statutory codification, Vishaka guidelines required institutions to prevent and redress workplace sexual harassment. The legal principle remains that employers must provide a safe and dignified workplace.

title: Free speech and online expression
citation: Shreya Singhal v. Union of India (2015)
text: Restrictions on speech must meet constitutional standards. Broad and vague criminalization of online expression was struck down where it disproportionately restricted free speech rights.

title: Arrest caution in matrimonial cruelty cases
citation: Arnesh Kumar v. State of Bihar (2014)
text: The Supreme Court cautioned against automatic arrests and emphasized statutory safeguards, requiring reasoned police action and judicial scrutiny instead of mechanical detention.

title: Landlord-tenant eviction due process
citation: State rent control and tenancy law principles in India
text: A landlord generally cannot forcibly evict a tenant without due legal process. Notice requirements, grounds of eviction, and procedure vary by state rent law and contract terms.

title: Contract breach and remedies
citation: Indian Contract Act, 1872 principles
text: When a contract is breached, remedies may include damages, specific performance in appropriate cases, injunctions, and restitution. Courts examine contract terms, proof of breach, mitigation efforts, and actual loss.

title: Cheque dishonour process
citation: Negotiable Instruments Act, 1881 (Section 138 framework)
text: Cheque dishonour proceedings typically require timely statutory notice and complaint filing within prescribed timelines. Missing limitation windows can affect enforceability.

title: Bail principles in criminal matters
citation: General Indian criminal procedure principles on bail
text: Bail decisions balance personal liberty with investigation and trial interests. Courts often consider gravity of offense, flight risk, witness tampering risk, and cooperation with process.

title: Cyber fraud complaint workflow
citation: Information Technology Act and criminal law complaint practice
text: Victims of online fraud should preserve transaction records, screenshots, account identifiers, and complaint numbers, then report promptly through cyber portals and police channels to improve recovery prospects.

title: Domestic violence civil protections
citation: Protection of Women from Domestic Violence Act, 2005
text: Affected persons may seek protection orders, residence orders, monetary relief, and custody-related directions through the statutory mechanism, in addition to applicable criminal remedies.
